title: Borrowing a Drill from the Neighbour
summary: Sara knocks on her neighbour Mr Klein's door in their old Berlin building to borrow a drill so she can hang a heavy picture frame.

body.speaker: Sara
body.text: Hello Mr Klein, sorry to bother you. Do you have a moment?

body.speaker: Mr Klein
body.text: Of course, Sara. Come in. Is everything all right?

body.speaker: Sara
body.text: Yes, everything's fine. I just wanted to ask if I could borrow your drill for an hour or two.

body.speaker: Mr Klein
body.text: My drill? Sure. What are you planning to drill?

body.speaker: Sara
body.text: I bought a heavy picture frame at the market last weekend. I'd like to hang it above the sofa, but the wall is solid concrete.

body.speaker: Mr Klein
body.text: Concrete is no problem. You'll need a special drill bit, though. Do you have one?

body.speaker: Sara
body.text: No, I don't. I only have small ones for wood.

body.speaker: Mr Klein
body.text: I'll lend you a six-millimetre concrete bit. It should be the right size for normal wall plugs.

body.speaker: Sara
body.text: Thank you so much. I've never used a drill on concrete before. Is it difficult?

body.speaker: Mr Klein
body.text: Not really. Push slowly, don't force the drill, and stop every few seconds. The dust can be quite a lot.

body.speaker: Sara
body.text: Should I cover the floor with something?

body.speaker: Mr Klein
body.text: An old newspaper or a sheet is perfect. And wear glasses if you have any. The dust can fly into your eyes.

body.speaker: Sara
body.text: Got it. I'll bring everything back tomorrow morning before work.

body.speaker: Mr Klein
body.text: No hurry. Just knock if you have a problem — I'm at home all weekend.

annotations.exampleEn: Sorry to bother you — could you sign this paper?

annotations.meaningVi: mượn cái máy khoan của anh
annotations.exampleEn: Can I borrow your drill this evening?
I dropped the heavy picture frame on my foot.
These walls are solid concrete, not plaster.
Use the right drill bit for the material.
Put the wall plugs in before the screws.
Don't force the drill — let it cut at its own speed.
There was dust all over the table.
Use an old newspaper to protect the floor.
The sand could fly into your eyes.

grammarNotes.title: 'Could I…?' and 'Can I…?' for asking permission
grammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Could I + V?' lịch sự hơn 'Can I + V?'. Dùng để xin phép một cách nhã nhặn. Trong bài: 'I just wanted to ask if I could borrow your drill.' (lịch sự, hơi gián tiếp).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use 'Could I + verb?' as a polite way to ask permission. 'Can I + verb?' is also fine but a bit less formal. From the dialogue: 'I just wanted to ask if I could borrow your drill.'

grammarNotes.title: Imperative form for instructions and advice
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng động từ nguyên thể (không có chủ ngữ) để đưa ra hướng dẫn. Thêm 'don't' để nói điều không nên làm. Trong bài: 'Push slowly', 'don't force the drill', 'stop every few seconds', 'wear glasses if you have any'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use the bare verb form for instructions and advice. Add 'don't' for negative instructions. From the dialogue: 'Push slowly, don't force the drill, and stop every few seconds.'

translationVi:
Sara: Chào bác Klein, xin lỗi vì làm phiền. Bác có chút thời gian không ạ?
Mr Klein: Tất nhiên rồi, Sara. Vào đi cháu. Có chuyện gì không?
Sara: Dạ không, mọi chuyện đều ổn. Cháu chỉ muốn hỏi liệu có thể mượn máy khoan của bác một hai tiếng được không.
Mr Klein: Máy khoan của bác à? Được chứ. Cháu định khoan cái gì?
Sara: Cháu mua một khung tranh nặng ở chợ cuối tuần trước. Cháu muốn treo nó phía trên ghế sofa, nhưng tường là bê tông đặc.
Mr Klein: Bê tông thì không sao. Nhưng cháu sẽ cần một loại mũi khoan đặc biệt. Cháu có không?
Sara: Dạ không. Cháu chỉ có vài cái nhỏ để khoan gỗ.
Mr Klein: Bác sẽ cho cháu mượn mũi khoan bê tông 6 mi-li-mét. Cỡ này hợp với nở tường bình thường.
Sara: Cảm ơn bác nhiều. Cháu chưa từng khoan bê tông bao giờ. Có khó không ạ?
Mr Klein: Cũng không hẳn. Cứ đẩy chậm, đừng ép máy khoan, và dừng lại sau mỗi vài giây. Bụi sẽ khá nhiều đấy.
Sara: Cháu có nên trải gì đó dưới sàn không?
Mr Klein: Tờ báo cũ hoặc một tấm vải là hợp lý. Và đeo kính nếu có. Bụi có thể bay vào mắt.
Sara: Cháu hiểu rồi. Cháu sẽ trả lại đồ vào sáng mai trước giờ đi làm.
Mr Klein: Không có gì gấp. Có vấn đề gì thì cứ gõ cửa — cuối tuần bác ở nhà cả.

questions.prompt: Why does Sara knock on Mr Klein's door?
options: To ask if she can borrow his drill
questions.explanation: 'I just wanted to ask if I could borrow your drill for an hour or two.'
questions.hint: Listen for what she wants to borrow.

questions.prompt: What does Sara want to hang on the wall?
questions.explanation: 'I bought a heavy picture frame at the market last weekend.'
questions.hint: Listen for what she bought at the market.

questions.prompt: What is her wall made of?
questions.hint: Listen for two words describing the wall material.

questions.prompt: What special item does she need besides the drill?
questions.explanation: 'You'll need a special drill bit, though.'
questions.hint: Listen for what Mr Klein says she'll need.

questions.prompt: What size drill bit does he offer her?
questions.explanation: 'I'll lend you a six-millimetre concrete bit.'
questions.hint: Listen for a number followed by millimetres.

questions.prompt: Has Sara ever used a drill on concrete before?
questions.explanation: 'I've never used a drill on concrete before.'
questions.hint: Listen for what she has or hasn't done before.

questions.prompt: What three pieces of advice does Mr Klein give for using the drill?
options: Push fast, force the drill, never stop
Push slowly, don't force the drill, stop every few seconds
Stand far back, hold one hand, sing loudly
Use both hands, close your eyes, push hard
questions.explanation: 'Push slowly, don't force the drill, and stop every few seconds.'
questions.hint: Listen for three short instructions in one line.

questions.prompt: What does Mr Klein suggest she put on the floor?
options: An old newspaper or a sheet
questions.explanation: 'An old newspaper or a sheet is perfect.'
questions.hint: Listen for one of two flat items.

questions.prompt: Why does he suggest wearing glasses?
options: Because the wall is hard to see
Because the dust can fly into your eyes
questions.explanation: 'The dust can fly into your eyes.'

questions.prompt: When will Sara bring the drill back?
questions.explanation: 'I'll bring everything back tomorrow morning before work.'
questions.hint: Listen for a time of day.

questions.prompt: How can you describe Mr Klein's attitude in this dialogue?
questions.explanation: He gives her clear advice and says 'No hurry. Just knock if you have a problem.'
questions.hint: Think about how he speaks to Sara overall.

cloze.template:
Sara: Hello Mr Klein, sorry to {{b1}} you. Do you have a moment?
Mr Klein: Of course, Sara. Come in. Is everything all right?
Sara: Yes, everything's fine. I just wanted to ask if I could {{b2}} your drill for an hour or two.
Mr Klein: My drill? Sure. What are you planning to drill?
Sara: I bought a heavy picture {{b3}} at the market last weekend. I'd like to hang it above the sofa, but the wall is solid {{b4}}.
Mr Klein: Concrete is no problem. You'll need a special drill {{b5}}, though. Do you have one?
Sara: No, I don't. I only have small ones for wood.
Mr Klein: I'll lend you a six-millimetre concrete bit. It should be the right size for normal wall {{b6}}.
Sara: Thank you so much. I've never used a drill on concrete before. Is it difficult?
Mr Klein: Not really. Push slowly, don't {{b7}} the drill, and stop every few seconds. The dust can be quite a lot.
Sara: Should I cover the floor with something?
Mr Klein: An old {{b8}} or a sheet is perfect. And wear glasses if you have any. The dust can fly into your eyes.
Sara: Got it. I'll bring everything back tomorrow morning before {{b9}}.
Mr Klein: No hurry. Just {{b10}} if you have a problem — I'm at home all weekend.

blanks.explanation: 'bother' — 'sorry to bother you' is a polite way to interrupt.
'borrow' — to take something for a short time and give it back.
'frame' — a picture frame holds an image.
'concrete' — the building material of the wall.
'bit' — the small metal part that cuts into the wall.
'plugs' — wall plugs hold the screws in the hole.
'force' — don't push the drill too hard.
'newspaper' — a cheap, easy cover for the floor.
'work' — she will return the drill before going to work.
'knock' — to tap on the door to call someone.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Sara borrows a drill from a neighbour instead of buying her own. In many cities people live next to each other for years without speaking. What can we gain — or lose — when we ask neighbours for small favours like this?
